title: Executive Brief
subtitle: Sutton SignWriting as research infrastructure
summary: A concise forwardable presentation for PI and institutional conversations about Sutton SignWriting as existing infrastructure that needs durable support.
synopsis: A short executive framing of the infrastructure case. It explains what Sutton SignWriting is, why its existing asset base matters, what support gap remains, and what a PI or host institution would unlock by giving the work a stable research home.

slides.title: Sutton SignWriting as Research Infrastructure
slides.summary: The most useful institutional framing is that Sutton SignWriting is already becoming research infrastructure, not merely a writing-system project.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for executive brief overview.
bullets: Writing system plus technical ecosystem
PI-facing and institution-facing framing
Forwardable summary of the larger initiative
slides.speech: The infrastructure case can be stated quickly. Sutton SignWriting is not only a writing system. It is emerging research infrastructure. That framing matters because it fits the kinds of conversations a PI, lab, or institutional home can act on.
slides.notes: Open simply and clearly so the framing lands fast.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Writing System] --> C[Research Infrastructure]
  B[Technical Ecosystem] --> C
  C --> D[PI or Institutional Home]

slides.title: What Sutton SignWriting Is
slides.summary: SignWriting is a structured visual script for signed languages, used internationally and supported by real tools, encodings, and data.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what Sutton SignWriting is.
bullets: Modality-independent and visually structured
Expressive enough for full signed languages
Used in education, research, and community contexts
Not a glossing or transcription layer on spoken language
slides.speech: Sutton SignWriting is a system for writing signed languages using a structured visual script. It is modality-independent, expressive enough for full signed languages, and already used internationally in education, research, and community settings. Unlike glossing or transcription systems, it is a direct written representation of signed language.
slides.notes: This is the definitional slide. Keep the distinction from glossing clear because it matters for academic readers.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Signed Language] --> B[Direct Written Representation]
  B --> C[Education]
  B --> D[Research]
  B --> E[Community Use]

slides.title: Existing Asset Base
slides.summary: The ecosystem already includes domains, tools, standards, code, and datasets under ongoing technical stewardship.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for existing SignWriting asset base.
bullets: Long-running domains and public sites
Production tools such as SignPuddle and SignMaker
ISWA 2010, FSW, SWU, fonts, and libraries
Datasets, dictionaries, and written materials
slides.speech: The existing asset base is one of the strongest reasons to take this seriously. The ecosystem already includes public domains, production tools, formal encoding standards, fonts, software libraries, packages, and accumulated datasets and written materials. This is not a speculative or early-stage system. It is a mature distributed platform developed over more than two decades.
slides.notes: Say directly that this already exists.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Domains and Sites] --> F[Existing Asset Base]
  B[Tools and Applications] --> F
  C[Standards and Encodings] --> F
  D[Code and Libraries] --> F
  E[Datasets and Dictionaries] --> F

slides.title: The Current Gap
slides.summary: The main problem is not missing ideas or missing software, but missing durable coordinated support for infrastructure that already exists.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the current gap.
bullets: Distributed maintenance across individuals
Uneven documentation and onboarding
Limited institutional anchoring
Fragile continuity for long-term stewardship
slides.speech: What is missing is not the idea, the community, or the software. What is missing is durable coordinated support. At present the ecosystem depends on distributed maintenance, uneven documentation, and limited institutional anchoring. The gap is not absence of infrastructure. The gap is absence of durable support for infrastructure that is already active.
slides.notes: This is the key transition slide. Keep the contrast clean: not missing idea, missing support.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Existing Infrastructure] --> B[Limited Institutional Support]
  B --> C[Fragile Continuity]
  C --> D[Risk to Preservation, Tools, and Research Use]

slides.title: Why This Matters for Research
slides.summary: Infrastructure turns isolated efforts into scalable programs across corpora, standards, computation, and visual-language research.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for research value of infrastructure.
bullets: Corpora and reproducible datasets
Machine learning and computational linguistics
Visual language processing and literacy research
Cross-linguistic comparison and documentation
slides.speech: Positioning SignWriting as infrastructure changes what becomes possible. A stable infrastructure layer would support corpus development, reproducible datasets, machine learning and computational linguistics, research into visual language processing and literacy, and cross-linguistic comparison and documentation. Without infrastructure, these remain isolated efforts. With infrastructure, they become scalable research programs.
slides.notes: Move from stewardship to academic upside here. Use the phrase 'scalable research programs.'
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Stable Infrastructure] --> B[Corpora]
  A --> C[Reusable Datasets]
  A --> D[Computational Research]
  A --> E[Visual Language Studies]
  A --> F[Cross-Linguistic Documentation]

slides.title: Why Now
slides.summary: The timing is strong because interest in multimodal language work is growing while the SignWriting technical base is mature enough to become grant-ready infrastructure.
bullets: Growing multimodal and non-speech language interest
More emphasis on data sharing and reusable datasets
Maturing tooling around SignWriting
Visible but under-supported global user base
slides.speech: Several factors make this a timely opportunity. There is growing interest in multimodal and non-speech language processing, increased emphasis on data sharing and reusable datasets, maturing tooling around SignWriting, and a visible global user base that is still under-supported. This creates a narrow window where institutional support can convert existing work into stable, grant-ready infrastructure.
slides.notes: This is the timing slide. Keep it forward-looking and avoid sounding alarmist.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Research Interest] --> E[Why Now]
  B[Data Sharing Pressure] --> E
  C[Maturing Tooling] --> E
  D[Global User Base] --> E
  E --> F[Grant-Ready Infrastructure Opportunity]

slides.title: What a PI or Host Institution Would Unlock
slides.summary: A stable home would turn informal stewardship into formal infrastructure through documentation, grants, partnerships, and coordinated development.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what a PI or host institution would unlock.
bullets: Coordinated development of tools and standards
Systematic documentation and onboarding
Grant proposals grounded in real assets
Partnerships across linguistics, education, CS, and neuroscience
slides.speech: A host institution would be building on an existing technical and data foundation, not initiating a new system. With a stable home, the project could transition from informal stewardship to formal infrastructure. That would enable coordinated development, systematic documentation, grant preparation grounded in real assets, interdisciplinary partnerships, and long-term preservation planning.
slides.notes: Make the practical upside concrete enough for a PI to imagine ownership.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[PI or Host Institution] --> B[Institutional Anchoring]
  A --> C[Research Framing]
  A --> D[Grant Development]
  A --> E[Program Integration]
  B --> F[Formal Infrastructure]

slides.title: Closing
slides.summary: Sutton SignWriting already works as a globally used system; with the right home, it becomes infrastructure others can rely on and fund with confidence.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for executive brief closing.
bullets: Working global system
Software, data, and standards already exist
Durable institutional support is the missing layer
slides.speech: The most compelling framing is simple. Sutton SignWriting already exists as a working, globally used system with software, data, and standards. What it lacks is durable institutional support. With the right home, it becomes infrastructure that others can rely on, build on, and fund against with confidence.
slides.notes: End with the simplest version of the case so it feels easy to forward after hearing.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Working Global System] --> B[Durable Institutional Support]
  B --> C[Infrastructure Others Can Rely On]
  C --> D[Research, Funding, and Growth]
